gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper aims to computationally design improved variants of Arabidopsis thaliana ent-copalyl diphosphate synthase (ent-CPS) to enhance gibberellin biosynthesis, which could indirectly boost tanshinone production. The problem is addressed through in silico saturation mutagenesis, stability and binding affinity screening, and double mutant cycle analysis, without experimental validation.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the computational pipeline: SNAP2 generated 15,257 variants, screened to 455 with high effect scores, then filtered by stability (FoldX) and binding affinity (docking) to 6 single mutants. Double mutants were constructed and analyzed. Validation used known loss-of-function mutants.
process_signals: Initial library of 15,257 non-synonymous variants created by SNAP2
Screening isolated 455 mutants with potentially improved functions
Further screening based on stability, ligand affinity, and structure reduced to 6 single mutants
Double mutants constructed from these 6 and evaluated
Validation against experimentally verified mutants with reduced activity
methods: AutoDock Vina for docking and binding affinity (ΔΔG_BIND)
failures_or_limits: No experimental validation of predicted improved mutants
Validation only on loss-of-function mutants, not gain-of-function

section_gists.heading: 1 Introduction
section_gists.gist: Ent-CPS is key for gibberellin biosynthesis; its activity could be enhanced to increase tanshinone production. Rational enzyme redesign using in silico tools like SNAP2 can create smaller variant libraries than directed evolution. The study uses SNAP2 for saturation mutagenesis and multi-step screening.
process_signals: Biological motivation: improve ent-CPS to boost gibberellins and tanshinones
Hypothesis: mutations can improve catalytic properties
Constraint: no experimental validation, purely computational
Rationale for using SNAP2: high-throughput in silico mutagenesis with effect scores
failures_or_limits: No experimental data for improved mutants
Reliance on computational predictions

section_gists.heading: 2.1 Searching for A. thaliana Ent-CPS Variants Affecting Enzyme Functions
section_gists.gist: SNAP2 used with thresholds: effect score >85 and probability ≥91% to select non-neutral variants from 15,257 mutants. These thresholds reduce false positives.
process_signals: SNAP2 chosen over SIFT, PolyPhen-2 for better performance
Thresholds set to >85 effect score and ≥91% probability to ensure high confidence
Resulted in 455 variants selected
failures_or_limits: High thresholds may miss some functional variants

section_gists.heading: 2.2 Ligand Docking
section_gists.gist: Crystal structure 4LIX used; native ligand GGPS docked with AutoDock Vina. Best pose selected by lowest binding energy and key residue contacts. Binding energy and affinity calculated.
process_signals: Used crystal structure with non-native ligand removed
Docking to obtain initial complex for mutant analysis
Binding energy (ΔG_BIND) and affinity (K_A) computed
failures_or_limits: Docking accuracy depends on software and pose selection

section_gists.heading: 2.3 Single Mutant Preparation and Evaluation
section_gists.gist: 455 mutants from SNAP2 were subjected to FoldX mutagenesis. Stability screening: ΔΔG_FOLD < -0.50 kcal/mol. Then docking for ΔΔG_BIND < -0.50 kcal/mol. Secondary structure checked with PsiPred. Finally, interatomic distances measured with YASARA.
process_signals: Stepwise filtering: stability → binding → secondary structure → active site geometry
Stability categories: minor (-1.0 to -0.5), moderate (-2.0 to -1.0), strong (≤ -2.0) kcal/mol
Only mutants with ΔΔG_FOLD < -0.5 passed
Then ΔΔG_BIND < -0.5 required
Secondary structure changes eliminated mutants
Distance analysis for catalytic residues
failures_or_limits: FoldX more accurate for destabilizing mutations
Docking may not capture dynamic effects

section_gists.heading: 2.4 Validation of Variant Screening Methodology
section_gists.gist: Validation used 11 known loss-of-function mutants (e.g., H331A, D379A). They were run through the same pipeline to see if they would be filtered out.
process_signals: Validation set: mutants with experimentally reduced activity
Goal: ensure pipeline eliminates unfavorable mutants
No positive controls (improved mutants) available
methods: Same pipeline: SNAP2, FoldX, docking, PsiPred, distances
failures_or_limits: Only negative controls; cannot validate prediction of improved mutants
Pipeline may be too stringent or lenient

section_gists.heading: 2.5 Double Mutant Preparation and Double Mutant Cycle Analysis
section_gists.gist: Six single mutants that passed all tests were combined into 15 double mutants. Same quality tests applied. Double mutant cycle analysis performed to identify interactions (synergistic, antagonistic, additive) based on Mildvan's definitions.
process_signals: Double mutants constructed from all pairwise combinations of 6 singles
Screened with same stability, binding, structure tests
Cycle analysis to detect cooperative effects
metrics: ΔΔG_FOLD and ΔΔG_BIND for singles and doubles
failures_or_limits: Assumes additivity; non-additive effects may be misclassified
Small number of singles limits combinatorial space

section_gists.heading: 3.1 Stability of Single Mutants
section_gists.gist: SNAP2 produced 15,257 variants; 455 passed thresholds. FoldX stability screening: only 14 had ΔΔG_FOLD < -0.5 kcal/mol. PsiPred removed 5 with secondary structure changes, leaving 9. Of these, 3 moderate, 2 strong, 4 minor stabilizers.
process_signals: 455 variants from SNAP2
14 passed stability filter
5 eliminated by secondary structure changes
9 carried forward: 2 hot spots, 3 warm, 4 minor
failures_or_limits: Only 14 out of 455 showed stabilizing potential
Secondary structure changes removed 5, including some with good stability scores

section_gists.heading: 3.2 Changes in Ligand Free Energy of Binding Induced by Mutation
section_gists.gist: Of 9 mutants, 3 had ΔΔG_BIND > -0.5 (D377K, G422M, K778W) and were removed. Remaining 6 showed improved affinity (K_A 79.99–119.32 nM vs WT 292.88 nM). Distance analysis: D379 closer to substrate in mutants, N425 further. Only D379-substrate distance change significant.
process_signals: Binding filter removed 3 mutants
6 final single mutants: T114F, D336L, D377L, G422L, S597W, K778F
All improved affinity
Distance trends: D379 closer to GGPS, N425 further from D379
Only D379:OD2–GGPS:C15 distance statistically significant
failures_or_limits: Minor changes in ΔΔG_BIND
Distance differences mostly not significant

section_gists.heading: 3.3 Validation of the Single Mutants Screening Procedure
section_gists.gist: 11 known deleterious mutants tested. None passed SNAP2 threshold. Only D380A passed ΔΔG_FOLD test. ΔΔG_BIND removed 3. Distance changes observed but not significant. SNAP2 and ΔΔG_FOLD most efficient at eliminating unfavorable mutants.
process_signals: All 11 failed SNAP2 score >85
10 of 11 failed ΔΔG_FOLD < -0.5 (only D380A passed)
ΔΔG_BIND removed 3
Secondary structure change in 1 (E211A)
Distance changes not statistically significant
SNAP2 and ΔΔG_FOLD are key filters
failures_or_limits: Validation only on negative mutants; cannot confirm pipeline selects true positives
D380A passed stability filter despite being deleterious, indicating false positive risk

section_gists.heading: 3.4 Double Mutants and Double Mutant Cycle Analysis
section_gists.gist: 15 double mutants built from 6 singles. 7 passed all tests (T114F/D377L, T114F/S597W, D336L/S597W, D336L/K778F, D377L/G422L, D377L/S597W, D377L/K778F). All improved affinity. Cycle analysis on ΔΔG_FOLD: 4 synergistic, 3 antagonistic (2 very weak). On ΔΔG_BIND: all synergistic.
process_signals: 7 double mutants passed quality tests
All showed improved affinity (77.33–116.14 nM)
Stability: 3 moderate, 3 strong improvements
ΔΔG_FOLD cycle: synergistic (T114F/D377L, D336L/K778F, D377L/G422L, D377L/K778F) and antagonistic (T114F/S597W, D336L/S597W, D377L/S597W); two antagonistic very weak, possibly additive
ΔΔG_BIND cycle: all synergistic
failures_or_limits: Small number of double mutants
Antagonistic interactions may be misclassified due to small energy differences
No experimental validation

section_gists.heading: 4 Discussion
section_gists.gist: SNAP2 selected 455 from 15,257. FoldX stability: 14 mutants, 8 moderate/strong. FoldX accuracy: correlation 0.81, SD 1.0–1.78 kcal/mol, better for destabilizing. 5 removed by secondary structure changes. 6 final singles with improved binding. Distance trends suggest altered interactions. Validation eliminated all 11 deleterious mutants. Double mutants showed synergistic binding effects. Limitations: no experimental validation, FoldX bias, docking approximations.
process_signals: FoldX more accurate for destabilizing mutations
Secondary structure changes in C-terminal region may be due to less stabilization
Distance analysis: D379 closer to substrate, N425 weaker interaction in mutants
Hydrogen bond strengths categorized
Validation successful in eliminating negative controls
Double mutants all synergistic for binding
Acknowledged limitations: computational predictions need experimental verification
methods: SNAP2, FoldX, docking, PsiPred, distances, cycle analysis
failures_or_limits: No experimental validation of predicted improved mutants
FoldX SD 1.0–1.78 kcal/mol may misclassify borderline cases
Docking may not reflect true binding dynamics
Only negative controls for validation

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research follows a funnel-like computational screening strategy: start with all possible single mutants, filter by predicted functional impact (SNAP2), then by stability (FoldX), binding affinity (docking), structural integrity (PsiPred), and active site geometry (distances).
The hypothesis is that mutations improving stability and substrate binding will enhance catalytic activity, but this is not directly tested.
Double mutants are expected to combine beneficial effects, and cycle analysis reveals cooperative interactions.
Validation against known deleterious mutants confirms the pipeline can eliminate bad candidates, but does not prove it can identify good ones.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Do the predicted single and double mutants actually have improved catalytic activity in vitro or in vivo?
Are the computational predictions (SNAP2, FoldX, docking) accurate enough to reliably identify beneficial mutations?
How do the distance changes translate to catalytic efficiency?
Are the synergistic/antagonistic interactions in double mutants real or artifacts of the computational methods?
Can this pipeline be generalized to other enzymes?

events.summary: The paper addresses the need to improve ent-CPS activity to boost gibberellin and tanshinone production.
The study uses in silico saturation mutagenesis with SNAP2 to generate 15,257 non-synonymous variants of A. thaliana ent-CPS.
SNAP2 thresholds set to effect score >85 and probability ≥91% to select non-neutral variants.
455 mutants were selected from the initial 15,257 based on SNAP2 thresholds.
The 455 mutants were subjected to further screening based on stability, ligand affinity, secondary structure, and active site structure.
Six single mutants were obtained after all screening steps.
Double mutants were constructed from the six single mutants and evaluated by double mutant cycle analysis.
The screening procedure was validated using experimentally verified enzyme mutants with reduced enzymatic activity.

The next four double mutants (T114F/D377L, D336L/K778F, D377L/G422L, D377L/K778F) showed a synergistic effect on protein $\Delta\Delta G_{FOLD}$ , suggesting the presence of anti-cooperatively interacting residues.

In addition, the double mutant cycle analysis for $\Delta\Delta G_{FOLD}$ indicated the presence of antagonistic (T114F/S597W, D336L/S597W, D377L/S597W) interactions between aa residues constituting the double mutant.

As the antagonistic effect was very weak, it is possible that both double mutants (T114F/S597W, D336L/S597W) are in fact formed from single mutants, suggesting an additive effect takes place rather than an antagonistic one.

validation_methods: In silico validation using 11 known deleterious mutants
success_verification.final_conclusion: A computational pipeline identified 6 single mutants (T114F, D336L, D377L, G422L, S597W, K778F) and 7 double mutants with predicted improved stability and binding affinity for GGPS. The pipeline was validated by correctly rejecting 11 known deleterious mutants. However, no experimental validation was performed, and the D336L mutation may impair reprotonation of D379, potentially reducing catalytic activity.
